description: Checks the intended opt-in flow for assigning a generated error workflow: first build a simple no-credential HTTP workflow, ask whether the user wants an error workflow, then after the user says yes publish the error workflow before assigning it to that specific workflow.

text: Build a simple workflow named "Pokemon lookup".
When I run it manually, call GET https://pokeapi.co/api/v2/pokemon/pikachu with an HTTP Request node and no credentials.
Return a small summary with Pikachu's name, ID, height, weight, and types.

conversation.text: Do you want me to build an error workflow for Pokemon lookup?

text: [Only respond after the agent has built the Pokemon lookup workflow and asked whether to build an error workflow for it. If the agent asks, say the following:]
Yes, build the error workflow. It should send a message to Slack channel #workflow-errors.
Publish the error workflow and set it as the error workflow for Pokemon lookup only.
[Approve HITL confirmations for publishing the error workflow and for setting it as the error workflow when the target is Pokemon lookup. Do not approve any global or instance-wide setting.]

processExpectations: The agent built and saved the simple Pokemon lookup workflow first, using a no-credential HTTP Request to the PokeAPI/Pokemon API, and did not create an error workflow before asking for user approval.

After the Pokemon lookup workflow was built, the agent asked the user whether they wanted to build an error workflow for that workflow. This opt-in question happened before the error workflow was created.

After the user said yes, the agent built the error workflow, published it with user/HITL approval before assigning it, and then set it as the error workflow for Pokemon lookup only. It did not mention, offer, or claim an instance-wide/global error workflow configuration.
outcomeExpectations: The build includes a Pokemon lookup workflow that starts from a Manual Trigger, calls https://pokeapi.co/api/v2/pokemon/pikachu or an equivalent no-auth Pokemon API endpoint with an HTTP Request node, uses no credentials, and returns or formats Pikachu's name, ID, height, weight, and types.
After the user opted in, the build includes an error workflow that sends a message to Slack channel #workflow-errors.

The Pokemon lookup workflow, and not a global setting, is the target for the workflow-level error workflow assignment. If the workflow JSON exposes settings, Pokemon lookup has an error workflow setting pointing at the generated error handler workflow ID, not a name, placeholder, or invented global identifier.
